source: Invest Like the Best (YouTube) — Watts and wafers
summary: Cross-sectional AI valuations are deeply inconsistent — semi-cap equipment at 40x annualized earnings vs. DRAM at mid-single digit P/E cannot both be correct
summary_extended: Baker argues that current cross-sectional valuations across the semiconductor supply chain are deeply inconsistent and cannot simultaneously be correct. Specifically, he notes that semi-cap equipment stocks are trading at approximately 40x annualized earnings while DRAM stocks trade at mid-single-digit P/Es — a stark contrast to the prior cycle peak where semi-cap traded at ~5x and DRAM at ~12x, meaning the historical relationship has essentially inverted. He contends this gap is unjustifiable even after accounting for improved semi-cap business model quality and uncertainty around HBM demand, and that one or both asset classes must reprice. He further highlights a second internal inconsistency: Nvidia's valuation appears cheap relative to its own history, while GE Vernova's valuation implies power demand growth so extreme it would require an unfathomable loss of Nvidia GPU market share — these two valuations cannot coexist. The investment implication is to be long DRAM (particularly Micron as the US-listed pure play) and skeptical of semi-cap equipment multiples, while the Nvidia/GEV inconsistency suggests either GEV is overvalued or NVDA is undervalued.
detail: Baker notes that at the peak of the last cycle, semi-cap equipment traded at ~5x earnings vs. DRAM at ~12x. Currently semi-cap is at ~40x vs. DRAM at mid-single digit. He argues this gap is unjustifiable even accounting for improved semi-cap business models and HBM uncertainty. He also flags the inconsistency between Nvidia's valuation (cheap relative to history) and GE Vernova's valuation (implying unfathomable Nvidia share loss).
confidence_evidence: Baker provides specific valuation data points (40x vs. mid-single digit, historical 5x vs. 12x) and frames this as a clear mispricing he is acting on
quote_fragment: semi-cap at 40x next quarter's earnings and DRAM at mid-single digit — can't both be true

ticker_audit.reasoning: Baker explicitly uses Nvidia's valuation as one of the two legs of the GEV/NVDA inconsistency argument — it is a direct subject of this thesis, not tangential.
Baker explicitly flags GE Vernova's valuation as implying an unfathomable Nvidia share loss, making it a direct subject of the valuation inconsistency thesis.

ASML is the dominant semi-cap equipment company and is directly implicated in the 40x semi-cap equipment valuation argument — it is a subject of the overvaluation claim, not merely tangential. [RE-AUDIT: Baker made a general claim about semi-cap equipment trading at ~40x annualized earnings as a sector-level observation. He did not specifically name ASML as the subject of this claim. Inferring ASML because it is the dominant semi-cap company is exactly the kind of sector-level inference the audit rules prohibit.]

LRCX was not specifically named as a subject of this thesis; including it is a sector-level inference that the audit rules prohibit.]

Micron is the only US-listed pure-play DRAM company and is the primary investable expression of Baker's long DRAM thesis given mid-single-digit P/E; clearly implied as the actionable long.
